data.title: Teva (TEVA) Is Up 6.0% After Q3 Profitability Return and Updated AUSTEDO Guidance Has the Bull Case Changed?
data.description:

data.title: KT (KRX:030200) Has Some Way To Go To Become A Multi-Bagger
data.description:
Finding a business that has the potential to grow substantially is not easy, but it is possible if we look at a few key financial metrics. Amongst other things, we'll want to see two things; firstly, a growing return on capital employed (ROCE) and secondly, an expansion in the company's amount of capital employed. This shows us that it's a compounding machine, able to continually reinvest its earnings back into the business and generate higher returns. However, after investigating KT (KRX:030200), we don't think it's current trends fit the mold of a multi-bagger.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. To calculate this metric for KT, this is the formula:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.052 = ₩1.6t ÷ (₩43t - ₩12t) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

So, KT has an ROCE of 5.2%. Ultimately, that's a low return and it under-performs the Telecom industry average of 11%.

Check out our latest analysis for KT

In the above chart we have measured KT's prior ROCE against its prior performance, but the future is arguably more important. If you'd like, you can check out the forecasts from the analysts covering KT for free.

What The Trend Of ROCE Can Tell Us

The returns on capital haven't changed much for KT in recent years. Over the past five years, ROCE has remained relatively flat at around 5.2% and the business has deployed 26% more capital into its operations. Given the company has increased the amount of capital employed, it appears the investments that have been made simply don't provide a high return on capital.

Our Take On KT's ROCE

Long story short, while KT has been reinvesting its capital, the returns that it's generating haven't increased. Investors must think there's better things to come because the stock has knocked it out of the park, delivering a 191% gain to shareholders who have held over the last five years. But if the trajectory of these underlying trends continue, we think the likelihood of it being a multi-bagger from here isn't high.

On a final note, we've found 1 warning sign for KT that we think you should be aware of.

data.title: Is Now the Right Moment for First Solar Stock After 35% Jump in 2025?
data.description:
• Wondering if First Solar’s recent performance means the stock is a bargain or just riding a wave? You’re in the right place for a closer look at what the numbers (and a bit of context) might really be telling us.
  • First Solar’s stock has made some serious moves lately, climbing 35.7% year-to-date and 32.9% over the past year. However, it dipped 5.4% in the past week, which may signal that volatility could be as much an opportunity as a warning sign.
  • This past month, First Solar’s price shifts have been set against a backdrop of increased attention on clean energy policy in the US, as well as significant investments in solar infrastructure by major utility providers. Headlines about fresh government incentives and new project announcements have fueled both optimism and speculation around future revenue growth.
  • As for value, First Solar currently scores a 5 out of 6 on our valuation checks, which is an impressive mark that’s worth unpacking. We’ll walk through several approaches to valuation, but stick around, because by the end of this article you’ll see there’s an even deeper way to judge if the numbers really stack up.

Find out why First Solar's 32.9% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: First Solar Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model is a common approach used by analysts to estimate a company’s intrinsic value by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those amounts back to their present value. This gives investors a sense of what a company’s shares are truly worth, based on fundamental financial performance rather than just market sentiment.

For First Solar, the current Free Cash Flow sits at a negative $22.4 million, reflecting recent investments and spending. However, the DCF model projects strong growth ahead. Analyst forecasts estimate that Free Cash Flow will climb to $1.42 billion in 2026 and keep accelerating, reaching nearly $4.0 billion by 2029. Beyond that, Simply Wall St extrapolates continued increases, with the ten-year forecast topping $5.6 billion in 2035. All figures are in US dollars.

Based on these projections, the resulting intrinsic value for First Solar is estimated at $480 per share. This figure stands about 47.3% higher than the current share price and implies a substantial margin of undervaluation.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests First Solar is undervalued by 47.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 878 more undervalued stocks based on cash flows.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for First Solar.

Approach 2: First Solar Price vs Earnings (PE Ratio)

The price-to-earnings (PE) ratio is a widely used valuation metric, especially for profitable companies like First Solar. This ratio tells investors how much they are paying for each dollar of earnings, making it easier to compare companies across industries and historical periods.

The "right" or "fair" PE ratio for a stock depends on factors such as expected earnings growth, profit margins, and perceived risk. Fast-growing or lower-risk companies typically trade at higher PE ratios, while slower-growing or riskier firms tend to command lower multiples.

Currently, First Solar trades at a PE ratio of 19.4x. This compares favorably to the average PE across the semiconductor industry, which stands at 34.2x, and to its peer group average of 37.8x. On the surface, this would hint that First Solar is attractively priced relative to these benchmarks.

However, Simply Wall St’s "Fair Ratio" model takes this a step further by incorporating more nuanced data such as projected growth, risk profile, profit margins, and company size. This results in a Fair Ratio of 38.0x for First Solar. Unlike a blunt industry average, the Fair Ratio drills down into what is appropriate given First Solar’s specific fundamentals and prospects.

When comparing the Fair Ratio of 38.0x to the company’s current 19.4x, First Solar looks significantly undervalued on this basis, suggesting the market may not be fully appreciating its earnings power and long-term growth outlook.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your First Solar Narrative

Earlier we mentioned there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let’s introduce you to Narratives. This is a smarter, more dynamic approach to making investment decisions. A Narrative is simply your story about a company’s future: it connects your expectations for important numbers like revenue, earnings, and margins to a clear financial forecast and, ultimately, to your fair value estimate.

Unlike traditional models, Narratives help you see beyond the numbers by letting you explain your assumptions and reasoning. Best of all, Narratives are not static. If new news or earnings arrive, your narrative’s fair value refreshes, keeping your decisions up to date as conditions change.
